     2	/** @import { AST } from '#compiler' */
     3	/** @import { ComponentClientTransformState, ComponentContext } from '../types' */
     4	import { TEMPLATE_FRAGMENT, TEMPLATE_USE_IMPORT_NODE } from '../../../../../constants.js';
     5	import * as b from '#compiler/builders';
     6	import { clean_nodes, infer_namespace } from '../../utils.js';
     7	import { transform_template } from '../transform-template/index.js';
     8	import { process_children } from './shared/fragment.js';
     9	import { build_render_statement, Memoizer } from './shared/utils.js';
    10	import { Template } from '../transform-template/template.js';
    11	
    12	/**
    13	 * @param {AST.Fragment} node
    14	 * @param {ComponentContext} context
    15	 */
    16	export function Fragment(node, context) {
    17	        // Creates a new block which looks roughly like this:
    18	        // ```js
    19	        // // hoisted:
    20	        // const block_name = $.from_html(`...`);
    21	        //
    22	        // // for the main block:
    23	        // const id = block_name();
    24	        // // init stuff and possibly render effect
    25	        // $.append($$anchor, id);
    26	        // ```
    27	        // Adds the hoisted parts to `context.state.hoisted` and returns the statements of the main block.
    28	
    29	        const parent = context.path.at(-1) ?? node;
    30	
    31	        const namespace = infer_namespace(context.state.metadata.namespace, parent, node.nodes);
    32	
    33	        const { hoisted, trimmed, is_standalone, is_text_first } = clean_nodes(
    34	                parent,
    35	                node.nodes,
    36	                context.path,
    37	                namespace,
    38	                context.state,
    39	                context.state.preserve_whitespace,
    40	                context.state.options.preserveComments
    41	        );
    42	
    43	        if (hoisted.length === 0 && trimmed.length === 0) {
    44	                return b.block([]);
    45	        }
    46	
    47	        const is_single_element = trimmed.length === 1 && trimmed[0].type === 'RegularElement';
    48	        const is_single_child_not_needing_template =
    49	                trimmed.length === 1 &&
    50	                (trimmed[0].type === 'SvelteFragment' || trimmed[0].type === 'TitleElement');
    51	        const has_await = context.state.init !== null && (node.metadata.has_await || false);
    52	
    53	        const template_name = context.state.scope.root.unique('root'); // TODO infer name from parent
    54	
    55	        /** @type {Statement[]} */
    56	        const body = [];
    57	
    58	        /** @type {Statement | undefined} */
    59	        let close = undefined;
    60	
    61	        /** @type {ComponentClientTransformState} */
    62	        const state = {
    63	                ...context.state,
    64	                init: [],
    65	                consts: [],
    66	                update: [],
    67	                after_update: [],
    68	                memoizer: new Memoizer(),
    69	                template: new Template(),
    70	                transform: { ...context.state.transform },
    71	                metadata: {
    72	                        namespace,
    73	                        bound_contenteditable: context.state.metadata.bound_contenteditable
    74	                }
    75	        };
    76	
    77	        for (const node of hoisted) {
    78	                context.visit(node, state);
    79	        }
    80	
    81	        if (is_single_element) {
    82	                const element = /** @type {AST.RegularElement} */ (trimmed[0]);
    83	
    84	                const id = b.id(context.state.scope.generate(element.name));
    85	
    86	                context.visit(element, {
    87	                        ...state,
    88	                        node: id
    89	                });
    90	
    91	                let flags = state.template.needs_import_node ? TEMPLATE_USE_IMPORT_NODE : undefined;
    92	
    93	                const template = transform_template(state, namespace, flags);
    94	                state.hoisted.push(b.var(template_name, template));
    95	
    96	                state.init.unshift(b.var(id, b.call(template_name)));
    97	                close = b.stmt(b.call('$.append', b.id('$$anchor'), id));
    98	        } else if (is_single_child_not_needing_template) {
    99	                context.visit(trimmed[0], state);
   100	        } else if (trimmed.length === 1 && trimmed[0].type === 'Text') {
   101	                const id = b.id(context.state.scope.generate('text'));
   102	                state.init.unshift(b.var(id, b.call('$.text', b.literal(trimmed[0].data))));
   103	                close = b.stmt(b.call('$.append', b.id('$$anchor'), id));
   104	        } else if (trimmed.length > 0) {
   105	                const id = b.id(context.state.scope.generate('fragment'));
   106	
   107	                const use_space_template =
   108	                        trimmed.some((node) => node.type === 'ExpressionTag') &&
   109	                        trimmed.every((node) => node.type === 'Text' || node.type === 'ExpressionTag');
   110	
   111	                if (use_space_template) {
   112	                        // special case — we can use `$.text` instead of creating a unique template
   113	                        const id = b.id(context.state.scope.generate('text'));
   114	
   115	                        process_children(trimmed, () => id, false, {
   116	                                ...context,
   117	                                state
   118	                        });
   119	
   120	                        state.init.unshift(b.var(id, b.call('$.text')));
   121	                        close = b.stmt(b.call('$.append', b.id('$$anchor'), id));
   122	                } else {
   123	                        if (is_standalone) {
   124	                                // no need to create a template, we can just use the existing block's anchor
   125	                                process_children(trimmed, () => b.id('$$anchor'), false, { ...context, state });
   126	                        } else {
   127	                                /** @type {(is_text: boolean) => Expression} */
   128	                                const expression = (is_text) => b.call('$.first_child', id, is_text && b.true);
   129	
   130	                                process_children(trimmed, expression, false, { ...context, state });
   131	
   132	                                let flags = TEMPLATE_FRAGMENT;
   133	
   134	                                if (state.template.needs_import_node) {
   135	                                        flags |= TEMPLATE_USE_IMPORT_NODE;
   136	                                }
   137	
   138	                                if (state.template.nodes.length === 1 && state.template.nodes[0].type === 'comment') {
   139	                                        // special case — we can use `$.comment` instead of creating a unique template
   140	                                        state.init.unshift(b.var(id, b.call('$.comment')));
   141	                                } else {
   142	                                        const template = transform_template(state, namespace, flags);
   143	                                        state.hoisted.push(b.var(template_name, template));
   144	
   145	                                        state.init.unshift(b.var(id, b.call(template_name)));
   146	                                }
   147	
   148	                                close = b.stmt(b.call('$.append', b.id('$$anchor'), id));
   149	                        }
   150	                }
   151	        }
   152	
   153	        body.push(...state.consts);
   154	
   155	        if (has_await) {
   156	                body.push(b.if(b.call('$.aborted'), b.return()));
   157	        }
   158	
   159	        if (is_text_first) {
   160	                // skip over inserted comment
   161	                body.push(b.stmt(b.call('$.next')));
   162	        }
   163	
   164	        body.push(...state.init);
   165	
   166	        if (state.update.length > 0) {
   167	                body.push(build_render_statement(state));
   168	        }
   169	
   170	        body.push(...state.after_update);
   171	
   172	        if (close !== undefined) {
   173	                // It's important that close is the last statement in the block, as any previous statements
   174	                // could contain element insertions into the template, which the close statement needs to
   175	                // know of when constructing the list of current inner elements.
   176	                body.push(close);
   177	        }
   178	
   179	        if (has_await) {
   180	                return b.block([b.stmt(b.call('$.async_body', b.arrow([], b.block(body), true)))]);
   181	        } else {
   182	                return b.block(body);
   183	        }
   184	}

Now I see something important! In the Fragment visitor, when `has_await` is true, the body is wrapped in:

```js
b.block([b.stmt(b.call('$.async_body', b.arrow([], b.block(body), true)))])
```

Note that `b.arrow([], b.block(body), true)` creates an async arrow function with the body. Then `$.async_body` is called with this async function.

But wait, `$.async_body` is an async function! It returns a Promise. But the result of `$.async_body` is not awaited or returned. This is a statement, not a return.

But that's the Fragment case. In the SnippetBlock case, the snippet function is made async, and then inside it, there's a call to `$.async_body`:

```js
const mySnippet = async ($$anchor) => {
    $.async_body(async () => {
        // ...
    });
};
```

The `$.async_body` returns a Promise, but it's not awaited. This means the snippet function returns immediately, and the code inside `async_body` runs asynchronously.

But actually, looking at the SnippetBlock visitor again, it doesn't use `$.async_body`. The `$.async_body` in the snippet output comes from the Fragment visitor (which wraps the snippet's body). 

Let me trace through the code path for the snippet:
1. SnippetBlock visits the body with `context.visit(node.body, child_state)`
2. The body is a Fragment
3. Fragment visitor checks `has_await` and wraps in `$.async_body`

So the snippet becomes:
```js
const mySnippet = async ($$anchor) => {
    // This is the body from SnippetBlock:
    b.block([
        ...declarations,
        ...block  // block comes from context.visit(node.body)
    ])
};
```

Where `block` from `context.visit(node.body)` is the Fragment's output, which is wrapped in `$.async_body` if has_await is true.

So the full picture:
- SnippetBlock makes the snippet function async (because body has await)
- The snippet body (Fragment) wraps its content in `$.async_body` (because it has await)

Now, `$.async_body` is async and returns a Promise. But inside the async snippet function, this Promise is not awaited. So the snippet function returns immediately.

But wait, there's also the issue of `$.async_derived` being called. `async_derived` requires an active effect. If there's no active effect when the snippet is called, it throws "async_derived_orphan".

Let me check if there's an active effect when `mySnippet($$anchor)` is called. Looking at the output:

```js
export default function Test($$anchor) {
    const mySnippet = async ($$anchor) => {
        $.async_body(async () => {
            const value = (await $.save($.async_derived(() => promise)))();
            // ...
        });
    };
    
    let promise = $.proxy(Promise.resolve('hello'));
    
    mySnippet($$anchor);
}
```

`mySnippet` is called directly in the component function. There's no active effect at this point. So `$.async_body` is called, which calls `suspend()`. But `async_body` starts by getting `active_effect`:

```js
export async function async_body(fn) {
    const unsuspend = suspend();
    const active = /** @type {Effect} */ (active_effect);
    try {
        await fn();
    } catch (error) {
        if (!aborted(active)) {
            invoke_error_boundary(error, active);
        }
    } finally {
        unsuspend();
    }
}
```

If `active_effect` is null, then `active` is null, and `aborted(null)` might cause issues. But `aborted` now accepts an optional parameter:

```js
export function aborted(effect = /** @type {Effect} */ (active_effect)) {
    return (effect.f & DESTROYED) !== 0;
}
```

If `effect` is null, then `effect.f` would throw "Cannot read properties of null (reading 'f')". But that's a different error.

Actually wait, let me look at the flow more carefully. The component function is called during mounting, which happens inside an effect. So `active_effect` should be set.

But the issue reported is "value is not defined". Let me look at the `async_body` function implementation:

```js
export async function async_body(fn) {
    const unsuspend = suspend();
    const active = /** @type {Effect} */ (active_effect);
    try {
        await fn();
    } catch (error) {
        if (!aborted(active)) {
            invoke_error_boundary(error, active);
        }
    } finally {
        unsuspend();
    }
}
```

And then inside the snippet's async function:
```js
const value = (await $.save($.async_derived(() => promise)))();
```

Wait! `$.async_derived` is called, which creates an async effect. But `$.async_derived` expects `active_effect` to be non-null:

```js
export function async_derived(fn, location) {
    let parent = /** @type {Effect | null} */ (active_effect);
    if (parent === null) {
        e.async_derived_orphan();
    }
    // ...
}
```

If `active_effect` is null, it throws. But in the compiled code, `$.async_derived` is called inside `$.async_body`'s callback, which is called after `suspend()`. Does `suspend` affect `active_effect`?

   667	export function suspend() {
   668	        var boundary = get_pending_boundary();
   669	        var batch = /** @type {Batch} */ (current_batch);
   670	        var pending = boundary.pending;
   671	
   672	        boundary.update_pending_count(1);
   673	        if (!pending) batch.increment();
   674	
   675	        return function unsuspend() {
   676	                boundary.update_pending_count(-1);
   677	
   678	                if (!pending) {
   679	                        batch.activate();
   680	                        batch.decrement();
   681	                }
   682	
   683	                unset_context();
   684	        };
   685	}

Let me think about this differently. Maybe the "value is not defined" error is about the `value` variable in the generated code itself. Look at this generated code:

```js
const value = (await $.save($.async_derived(() => promise)))();
```

If `$.save` returns a function, then calling that function returns the source signal. That source is stored in `value`. Then `$.get(value)` reads it.

     1	/** @import { Pattern } from 'estree' */
     2	/** @import { AST } from '#compiler' */
     3	/** @import { ComponentContext } from '../types' */
     4	import { dev } from '../../../../state.js';
     5	import { extract_identifiers } from '../../../../utils/ast.js';
     6	import * as b from '#compiler/builders';
     7	import { create_derived } from '../utils.js';
     8	import { get_value } from './shared/declarations.js';
     9	import { build_expression } from './shared/utils.js';
    10	
    11	/**
    12	 * @param {AST.ConstTag} node
    13	 * @param {ComponentContext} context
    14	 */
    15	export function ConstTag(node, context) {
    16	        const declaration = node.declaration.declarations[0];
    17	        // TODO we can almost certainly share some code with $derived(...)
    18	        if (declaration.id.type === 'Identifier') {
    19	                const init = build_expression(
    20	                        { ...context, state: { ...context.state, in_derived: true } },
    21	                        declaration.init,
    22	                        node.metadata.expression
    23	                );
    24	
    25	                let expression = create_derived(context.state, init, node.metadata.expression.has_await);
    26	
    27	                if (dev) {
    28	                        expression = b.call('$.tag', expression, b.literal(declaration.id.name));
    29	                }
    30	
    31	                context.state.consts.push(b.const(declaration.id, expression));
    32	
    33	                context.state.transform[declaration.id.name] = { read: get_value };
    34	
    35	                // we need to eagerly evaluate the expression in order to hit any
    36	                // 'Cannot access x before initialization' errors
    37	                if (dev) {
    38	                        context.state.consts.push(b.stmt(b.call('$.get', declaration.id)));
    39	                }
    40	        } else {
    41	                const identifiers = extract_identifiers(declaration.id);
    42	                const tmp = b.id(context.state.scope.generate('computed_const'));
    43	
    44	                const transform = { ...context.state.transform };
    45	
    46	                // Make all identifiers that are declared within the following computed regular
    47	                // variables, as they are not signals in that context yet
    48	                for (const node of identifiers) {
    49	                        delete transform[node.name];
    50	                }
    51	
    52	                const child_state = /** @type {ComponentContext['state']} */ ({
    53	                        ...context.state,
    54	                        transform,
    55	                        in_derived: true
    56	                });
    57	
    58	                // TODO optimise the simple `{ x } = y` case — we can just return `y`
    59	                // instead of destructuring it only to return a new object
    60	                const init = build_expression(
    61	                        { ...context, state: child_state },
    62	                        declaration.init,
    63	                        node.metadata.expression
    64	                );
    65	
    66	                const block = b.block([
    67	                        b.const(/** @type {Pattern} */ (context.visit(declaration.id, child_state)), init),
    68	                        b.return(b.object(identifiers.map((node) => b.prop('init', node, node))))
    69	                ]);
    70	
    71	                let expression = create_derived(context.state, block, node.metadata.expression.has_await);
    72	
    73	                if (dev) {
    74	                        expression = b.call('$.tag', expression, b.literal('[@const]'));
    75	                }
    76	
    77	                context.state.consts.push(b.const(tmp, expression));
    78	
    79	                // we need to eagerly evaluate the expression in order to hit any
    80	                // 'Cannot access x before initialization' errors
    81	                if (dev) {
    82	                        context.state.consts.push(b.stmt(b.call('$.get', tmp)));
    83	                }
    84	
    85	                for (const node of identifiers) {
    86	                        context.state.transform[node.name] = {
    87	                                read: (node) => b.member(b.call('$.get', tmp), node)
    88	                        };
    89	                }
    90	        }
    91	}

     1	/** @import { ArrowFunctionExpression, AssignmentExpression, BlockStatement, Expression, FunctionDeclaration, FunctionExpression, Identifier, Node, Pattern, UpdateExpression } from 'estree' */
     2	/** @import { Binding } from '#compiler' */
     3	/** @import { ClientTransformState, ComponentClientTransformState, ComponentContext } from './types.js' */
     4	/** @import { Analysis } from '../../types.js' */
     5	/** @import { Scope } from '../../scope.js' */
     6	import * as b from '#compiler/builders';
     7	import { is_simple_expression } from '../../../utils/ast.js';
     8	import {
     9	        PROPS_IS_LAZY_INITIAL,
    10	        PROPS_IS_IMMUTABLE,
    11	        PROPS_IS_RUNES,
    12	        PROPS_IS_UPDATED,
    13	        PROPS_IS_BINDABLE
    14	} from '../../../../constants.js';
    15	import { dev } from '../../../state.js';
    16	import { walk } from 'zimmerframe';
    17	import { validate_mutation } from './visitors/shared/utils.js';
    18	
    19	/**
    20	 * @param {Binding} binding
    21	 * @param {Analysis} analysis
    22	 * @returns {boolean}
    23	 */
    24	export function is_state_source(binding, analysis) {
    25	        return (
    26	                (binding.kind === 'state' || binding.kind === 'raw_state') &&
    27	                (!analysis.immutable || binding.reassigned || analysis.accessors)
    28	        );
    29	}
    30	
    31	/**
    32	 * @param {Identifier} node
    33	 * @param {ClientTransformState} state
    34	 * @returns {Expression}
    35	 */
    36	export function build_getter(node, state) {
    37	        if (Object.hasOwn(state.transform, node.name)) {
    38	                const binding = state.scope.get(node.name);
    39	
    40	                // don't transform the declaration itself
    41	                if (node !== binding?.node) {
    42	                        return state.transform[node.name].read(node);
    43	                }
    44	        }
    45	
    46	        return node;
    47	}
    48	
    49	/**
    50	 * @param {FunctionDeclaration | FunctionExpression | ArrowFunctionExpression} node
    51	 * @param {ComponentContext} context
    52	 * @returns {Pattern[]}
    53	 */
    54	function get_hoisted_params(node, context) {
    55	        const scope = context.state.scope;
    56	
    57	        /** @type {Identifier[]} */
    58	        const params = [];
    59	
    60	        /**
    61	         * We only want to push if it's not already present to avoid name clashing
    62	         * @param {Identifier} id
    63	         */
    64	        function push_unique(id) {
    65	                if (!params.find((param) => param.name === id.name)) {
    66	                        params.push(id);
    67	                }
    68	        }
    69	
    70	        for (const [reference] of scope.references) {
    71	                let binding = scope.get(reference);
    72	
    73	                if (binding !== null && !scope.declarations.has(reference) && binding.initial !== node) {
    74	                        if (binding.kind === 'store_sub') {
    75	                                // We need both the subscription for getting the value and the store for updating
    76	                                push_unique(b.id(binding.node.name));
    77	                                binding = /** @type {Binding} */ (scope.get(binding.node.name.slice(1)));
    78	                        }
    79	
    80	                        let expression = context.state.transform[reference]?.read(b.id(binding.node.name));
    81	
    82	                        if (
    83	                                // If it's a destructured derived binding, then we can extract the derived signal reference and use that.
    84	                                // TODO this code is bad, we need to kill it
    85	                                expression != null &&
    86	                                typeof expression !== 'function' &&
    87	                                expression.type === 'MemberExpression' &&
    88	                                expression.object.type === 'CallExpression' &&
    89	                                expression.object.callee.type === 'Identifier' &&
    90	                                expression.object.callee.name === '$.get' &&
    91	                                expression.object.arguments[0].type === 'Identifier'
    92	                        ) {
    93	                                push_unique(b.id(expression.object.arguments[0].name));
    94	                        } else if (
    95	                                // If we are referencing a simple $$props value, then we need to reference the object property instead
    96	                                (binding.kind === 'prop' || binding.kind === 'bindable_prop') &&
    97	                                !is_prop_source(binding, context.state)
    98	                        ) {
    99	                                push_unique(b.id('$$props'));
   100	                        } else if (
   101	                                // imports don't need to be hoisted
   102	                                binding.declaration_kind !== 'import'
   103	                        ) {
   104	                                // create a copy to remove start/end tags which would mess up source maps
   105	                                push_unique(b.id(binding.node.name));
   106	                                // rest props are often accessed through the $$props object for optimization reasons,
   107	                                // but we can't know if the delegated event handler will use it, so we need to add both as params
   108	                                if (binding.kind === 'rest_prop' && context.state.analysis.runes) {
   109	                                        push_unique(b.id('$$props'));
   110	                                }
   111	                        }
   112	                }
   113	        }
   114	
   115	        if (dev) {
   116	                // this is a little hacky, but necessary for ownership validation
   117	                // to work inside hoisted event handlers
   118	
   119	                /**
   120	                 * @param {AssignmentExpression | UpdateExpression} node
   121	                 * @param {{ next: () => void, stop: () => void }} context
   122	                 */
   123	                function visit(node, { next, stop }) {
   124	                        if (validate_mutation(node, /** @type {any} */ (context), node) !== node) {
   125	                                params.push(b.id('$$ownership_validator'));
   126	                                stop();
   127	                        } else {
   128	                                next();
   129	                        }
   130	                }
   131	
   132	                walk(/** @type {Node} */ (node), null, {
   133	                        AssignmentExpression: visit,
   134	                        UpdateExpression: visit
   135	                });
   136	        }
   137	
   138	        return params;
   139	}
   140	
   141	/**
   142	 * @param {FunctionDeclaration | FunctionExpression | ArrowFunctionExpression} node
   143	 * @param {ComponentContext} context
   144	 * @returns {Pattern[]}
   145	 */
   146	export function build_hoisted_params(node, context) {
   147	        const hoisted_params = get_hoisted_params(node, context);
   148	        node.metadata.hoisted_params = hoisted_params;
   149	
   150	        /** @type {Pattern[]} */
   151	        const params = [];
   152	
   153	        if (node.params.length === 0) {
   154	                if (hoisted_params.length > 0) {
   155	                        // For the event object
   156	                        params.push(b.id(context.state.scope.generate('_')));
   157	                }
   158	        } else {
   159	                for (const param of node.params) {
   160	                        params.push(/** @type {Pattern} */ (context.visit(param)));
   161	                }
   162	        }
   163	
   164	        params.push(...hoisted_params);
   165	        return params;
   166	}
   167	
   168	/**
   169	 * @param {Binding} binding
   170	 * @param {ComponentClientTransformState} state
   171	 * @param {string} name
   172	 * @param {Expression | null} [initial]
   173	 * @returns
   174	 */
   175	export function get_prop_source(binding, state, name, initial) {
   176	        /** @type {Expression[]} */
   177	        const args = [b.id('$$props'), b.literal(name)];
   178	
   179	        let flags = 0;
   180	
   181	        if (binding.kind === 'bindable_prop') {
   182	                flags |= PROPS_IS_BINDABLE;
   183	        }
   184	
   185	        if (state.analysis.immutable) {
   186	                flags |= PROPS_IS_IMMUTABLE;
   187	        }
   188	
   189	        if (state.analysis.runes) {
   190	                flags |= PROPS_IS_RUNES;
   191	        }
   192	
   193	        if (
   194	                state.analysis.accessors ||
   195	                (state.analysis.immutable
   196	                        ? binding.reassigned || (state.analysis.runes && binding.mutated)
   197	                        : binding.updated)
   198	        ) {
   199	                flags |= PROPS_IS_UPDATED;
   200	        }
   201	
   202	        /** @type {Expression | undefined} */
   203	        let arg;
   204	
   205	        if (initial) {
   206	                // To avoid eagerly evaluating the right-hand-side, we wrap it in a thunk if necessary
   207	                if (is_simple_expression(initial)) {
   208	                        arg = initial;
   209	                } else {
   210	                        if (
   211	                                initial.type === 'CallExpression' &&
   212	                                initial.callee.type === 'Identifier' &&
   213	                                initial.arguments.length === 0
   214	                        ) {
   215	                                arg = initial.callee;
   216	                        } else {
   217	                                arg = b.thunk(initial);
   218	                        }
   219	
   220	                        flags |= PROPS_IS_LAZY_INITIAL;
   221	                }
   222	        }
   223	
   224	        if (flags || arg) {
   225	                args.push(b.literal(flags));
   226	                if (arg) args.push(arg);
   227	        }
   228	
   229	        return b.call('$.prop', ...args);
   230	}
   231	
   232	/**
   233	 *
   234	 * @param {Binding} binding
   235	 * @param {ClientTransformState} state
   236	 * @returns
   237	 */
   238	export function is_prop_source(binding, state) {
   239	        return (
   240	                (binding.kind === 'prop' || binding.kind === 'bindable_prop') &&
   241	                (!state.analysis.runes ||
   242	                        state.analysis.accessors ||
   243	                        binding.reassigned ||
   244	                        binding.initial ||
   245	                        // Until legacy mode is gone, we also need to use the prop source when only mutated is true,
   246	                        // because the parent could be a legacy component which needs coarse-grained reactivity
   247	                        binding.updated)
   248	        );
   249	}
   250	
   251	/**
   252	 * @param {Expression} node
   253	 * @param {Scope | null} scope
   254	 */
   255	export function should_proxy(node, scope) {
   256	        if (
   257	                !node ||
   258	                node.type === 'Literal' ||
   259	                node.type === 'TemplateLiteral' ||
   260	                node.type === 'ArrowFunctionExpression' ||
   261	                node.type === 'FunctionExpression' ||
   262	                node.type === 'UnaryExpression' ||
   263	                node.type === 'BinaryExpression' ||
   264	                (node.type === 'Identifier' && node.name === 'undefined')
   265	        ) {
   266	                return false;
   267	        }
   268	
   269	        if (node.type === 'Identifier' && scope !== null) {
   270	                const binding = scope.get(node.name);
   271	                // Let's see if the reference is something that can be proxied
   272	                if (
   273	                        binding !== null &&
   274	                        !binding.reassigned &&
   275	                        binding.initial !== null &&
   276	                        binding.initial.type !== 'FunctionDeclaration' &&
   277	                        binding.initial.type !== 'ClassDeclaration' &&
   278	                        binding.initial.type !== 'ImportDeclaration' &&
   279	                        binding.initial.type !== 'EachBlock' &&
   280	                        binding.initial.type !== 'SnippetBlock'
   281	                ) {
   282	                        return should_proxy(binding.initial, null);
   283	                }
   284	        }
   285	
   286	        return true;
   287	}
   288	
   289	/**
   290	 * Svelte legacy mode should use safe equals in most places, runes mode shouldn't
   291	 * @param {ComponentClientTransformState} state
   292	 * @param {Expression | BlockStatement} expression
   293	 * @param {boolean} [async]
   294	 */
   295	export function create_derived(state, expression, async = false) {
   296	        const thunk = b.thunk(expression, async);
   297	
   298	        if (async) {
   299	                return b.call(b.await(b.call('$.save', b.call('$.async_derived', thunk))));
   300	        } else {
   301	                return b.call(state.analysis.runes ? '$.derived' : '$.derived_safe_equal', thunk);
   302	        }
   303	}
   304

So `await $.save($.async_derived(...))` returns a function (the restore function from `save`), and then `()` calls it to get the value.

Now, where is the `value` variable coming from? Let me trace through the ConstTag visitor:

1. `ConstTag` is called with `{@const value = await promise}`
2. `declaration.id` is `value` (Identifier)
3. `declaration.init` is `await promise` (AwaitExpression)


`create_derived` returns:
```js

```

This is `(await $.save($.async_derived(thunk)))()`. The `b.call` has:
- callee: `b.await(b.call('$.save', b.call('$.async_derived', thunk)))` which is `await $.save($.async_derived(thunk))`
- arguments: none

So the full expression is `(await $.save($.async_derived(thunk)))()`.

Now, this is assigned to `const value`. But wait, there's a problem: `const value` is declared at the top level of the async snippet function. But the `await` is inside the async function. The `const value = (await ...)()` should work.

But what about `$.async_derived`? It requires `active_effect` to be non-null. Inside `$.async_body`, is `active_effect` still set?

Let me look at `async_body` again:
```js
export async function async_body(fn) {
    const unsuspend = suspend();
    const active = /** @type {Effect} */ (active_effect);
    try {
        await fn();
    } catch (error) {
        if (!aborted(active)) {
            invoke_error_boundary(error, active);
        }
    } finally {
        unsuspend();
    }
}
```

`async_body` captures `active_effect` at the beginning. Then `await fn()` is called. Inside `fn`, the code calls `$.async_derived`. But `async_derived` reads `active_effect` from the global state:

```js
export function async_derived(fn, location) {
    let parent = /** @type {Effect | null} */ (active_effect);
    if (parent === null) {
        e.async_derived_orphan();
    }
    // ...
}
```

So `active_effect` needs to still be set when `async_derived` is called. But `async_body` doesn't clear `active_effect`. It should still be set from the component initialization.

Hmm, but actually the issue might be different. Let me look at the generated code from my earlier test:

```js
const mySnippet = async ($$anchor) => {
    $.async_body(async () => {
        const value = (await $.save($.async_derived(() => promise)))();
        // ...
    });
};
```

Wait, `$.async_body` is NOT awaited! `mySnippet` calls `$.async_body` but doesn't await it. So `mySnippet` returns immediately (it's async, so it returns a Promise that resolves to undefined). The code inside `$.async_body` runs asynchronously.

But then, `mySnippet` is called from the component function without await:
```js

```

This means the component function continues immediately, and the snippet's content is rendered asynchronously. But the component function might return before the snippet's async content is resolved.

But more importantly, `$.async_body` is an async function. It returns a Promise. If it's not awaited, errors inside it might be unhandled Promise rejections.

The component function is called during mounting, which happens inside an effect. So `active_effect` should be set. But `mySnippet` is async and calls `$.async_body` which calls `suspend()`. Then inside the callback, `$.async_derived` is called. At this point, `active_effect` should still be set (since `suspend` doesn't clear it).

Actually wait, I just realized something. Let me look at the compiled output for the `await` inside the snippet more carefully. In my test:

```js
const value = (await $.save($.async_derived(() => promise)))();
```

But `$.async_derived` expects a function that returns a Promise. `() => promise` returns a Promise (since `promise` is `$state(Promise.resolve('hello'))`). But `promise` is a `$state`, so it should be accessed with `$.get(promise)` to get the actual Promise value. However, in the compiled code, it's `() => promise`, not `() => $.get(promise)`.

Wait, but `promise` is a `$state`. The transform for `$state` variables: if a `$state` variable is reassigned, it's transformed to `$.get(promise)`. But if it's never reassigned, the transform is removed (see the `_` visitor in transform-client.js):

```js
if (
    binding.kind === 'normal' ||
    (binding.kind === 'state' && !is_state_source(binding, state.analysis))
) {
    delete transform[name];
}
```

So for `$state` that is reassigned (like `promise` which is reassigned in the click handler), the transform should be active. But `() => promise` should be `() => $.get(promise)`.

```js

```

In the template effect version, it IS `() => $.get(promise)`. But in the const version:
```js
const value = (await $.save($.async_derived(() => promise)))();
```

It's `() => promise` (without `$.get`).

That's because the ConstTag uses `build_expression` which visits the expression. The `build_expression` function uses `context.visit(expression)`. The expression is just `promise` (the AwaitExpression's argument). When visiting `promise`, the Identifier visitor uses the transform to decide whether to wrap it with `$.get`.

     1	/** @import { Identifier, Node } from 'estree' */
     2	/** @import { Context } from '../types' */
     3	import is_reference from 'is-reference';
     4	import * as b from '#compiler/builders';
     5	import { build_getter } from '../utils.js';
     6	
     7	/**
     8	 * @param {Identifier} node
     9	 * @param {Context} context
    10	 */
    11	export function Identifier(node, context) {
    12	        const parent = /** @type {Node} */ (context.path.at(-1));
    13	
    14	        if (is_reference(node, parent)) {
    15	                if (node.name === '$$props') {
    16	                        return b.id('$$sanitized_props');
    17	                }
    18	
    19	                // Optimize prop access: If it's a member read access, we can use the $$props object directly
    20	                const binding = context.state.scope.get(node.name);
    21	                if (

`build_getter` uses `state.transform`:

```js
export function build_getter(node, state) {
    if (Object.hasOwn(state.transform, node.name)) {
        const binding = state.scope.get(node.name);
        if (node !== binding?.node) {
            return state.transform[node.name].read(node);
        }
    }
    return node;
}
```

So if `promise` has a transform, it should be applied. But let me check: does `promise` have a transform?

In the SnippetBlock, the `child_state` has `transform` copied from `context.state.transform`. The parent transform should include `promise` if it's a `$state` that is reassigned.

But wait, looking at the `_` visitor in transform-client.js:

```js
_: function set_scope(node, { next, state }) {
    const scope = state.scopes.get(node);
    if (scope && scope !== state.scope) {
        const transform = { ...state.transform };
        for (const [name, binding] of scope.declarations) {
            if (
                binding.kind === 'normal' ||
                (binding.kind === 'state' && !is_state_source(binding, state.analysis))
            ) {
                delete transform[name];
            }
        }
        next({ ...state, transform, scope });
    } else {
        next();
    }
},
```

When entering a new scope, it deletes transforms for variables that are declared in that scope (if they're `normal` or non-reassigned `state`). But `promise` is declared in the instance scope, not the snippet scope. So its transform should remain.

    99	export function call(callee, ...args) {
   100	        if (typeof callee === 'string') callee = id(callee);
   101	        args = args.slice();
   102	
   103	        // replacing missing arguments with `undefined`, unless they're at the end in which case remove them
   104	        let i = args.length;
   105	        let popping = true;
   106	        while (i--) {
   107	                if (!args[i]) {
   108	                        if (popping) {
   109	                                args.pop();
   110	                        } else {
   111	                                args[i] = id('undefined');

OK, that looks correct. So the generated code is:
```js
const value = (await $.save($.async_derived(() => promise)))();
```

This should work. But maybe the issue is that `$.async_derived` is called without an active effect, which throws "async_derived_orphan". But that would be a different error message.

Actually, wait. Let me re-read the error: "Uncaught (in promise) ReferenceError: value is not defined"

This could be happening at runtime when the code tries to access `value` but `value` hasn't been defined yet because the `await` hasn't resolved. But `const value = (await ...)()` should wait for the await before assigning.

Or maybe the issue is that the variable `value` is used somewhere else where it's not defined. Let me think about the scenario:

```svelte
{#snippet mySnippet()}
  {@const value = await promise}
  {value}
{/snippet}
```

The compiled code:
```js
const mySnippet = async ($$anchor) => {
    $.async_body(async () => {
        const value = (await $.save($.async_derived(() => promise)))();
        if ($.aborted()) return;
        $.next();
        var text = $.text();
        $.template_effect(() => $.set_text(text, $.get(value)));
        $.append($$anchor, text);
    });
};
```

Wait, `$.get(value)` - `value` is the result of `(await $.save($.async_derived(...)))()`. `$.save` returns a function that restores context and returns the value. But what value does it return?

`$.save`:
```js
export async function save(promise) {
    var restore = capture();
    var value = await promise;
    return () => {
        restore();
        return value;
    };
}
```

It takes a promise, awaits it, and returns a function that returns the resolved value. So `$.save($.async_derived(...))` awaits the promise from `async_derived`. 

`async_derived` returns `Promise<Source<V>>`. So the resolved value is a Source signal. Then `save` returns `() => { restore(); return value; }` where `value` is the Source signal.

Then `()` calls this function, which restores context and returns the Source.

Then `$.get(value)` reads the Source to get the actual value.

This seems correct, but there's a problem: `$.async_derived` needs an active effect. Let me check if there is one when this code runs.

When the component mounts:
1. `Test($$anchor)` is called
2. Inside, `mySnippet` is defined and called
3. `mySnippet` calls `$.async_body(async () => { ... })`
4. `$.async_body` calls `suspend()`, then `await fn()`
5. Inside `fn`, `$.async_derived(() => promise)` is called
6. `async_derived` checks `active_effect` - is it set?

At step 5, we're inside an async function that was called from `async_body`. The `active_effect` should still be set from the mounting process. But `async_body` is async - it uses `await fn()`. When we `await`, the JavaScript engine suspends execution and resumes later. At the point of resumption, `active_effect` might still be set (since it's a module-level variable, not affected by async/await).

But wait, `async_body` captures `active_effect` before the await:
```js
const active = /** @type {Effect} */ (active_effect);
try {
    await fn();
} catch (error) {
    if (!aborted(active)) {
        invoke_error_boundary(error, active);
    }
}
```

So `active` is captured before the await. But `$.async_derived` reads `active_effect` from the global variable, not from the captured `active`. So if something else changes `active_effect` between the `await` and the call to `$.async_derived`, it could be null.

But that seems unlikely for the initial mount.

OK, I'm going to take a step back and look at this more systematically. Let me check the tests to see if there are any failing tests related to this issue:

Actually, let me look at the `SnippetBlock` transform again. When the snippet body has `has_await = true`, the snippet function is made async. But what about the `declarations` in the snippet body? The SnippetBlock does:

```js
const block = /** @type {BlockStatement} */ (context.visit(node.body, child_state)).body;
body = b.block([
    dev ? b.stmt(b.call('$.validate_snippet_args', b.spread(b.id('arguments')))) : b.empty,
    ...declarations,
    ...block
]);
```

The `block` from `context.visit(node.body)` is the Fragment output. If the Fragment has `has_await`, it wraps its body in `$.async_body`. But the `declarations` (for destructured parameters) are placed BEFORE the `block`. So the structure is:

```js
async ($$anchor, ...) => {
    // declarations (for destructured params)
    // block (from Fragment, which may be wrapped in $.async_body)
}
```

If the Fragment wraps its content in `$.async_body`, then the declarations are outside the `async_body` wrapper. That could be an issue if the declarations need to be async too. But the declarations are for destructured parameters, which shouldn't involve await.

Maybe the issue is about how SvelteKit handles the `data` prop from `load` functions? In SvelteKit, you can do:

```svelte
<script>
  let { data } = $props();

  {await data.promise}

```

But that would be a `$props()` variable, which might be handled differently.

Actually, let me look at this from yet another angle. Maybe the issue is about hoisted snippets. When a snippet is hoisted to module level, it can't access instance variables. But what if the snippet references an awaited variable that's only available in the instance?

Let me check the hoisting logic. In SnippetBlock:

```js
// Top-level snippets are hoisted so they can be referenced in the `<script>`
if (context.path.length === 1 && context.path[0].type === 'Fragment') {
    if (node.metadata.can_hoist) {
        context.state.module_level_snippets.push(declaration);
    } else {
        context.state.instance_level_snippets.push(declaration);
    }
} else {
    context.state.init.push(declaration);
}
```

The `can_hoist` is determined by `can_hoist_snippet` function which checks if all references are to bindings at function depth 0 (module scope) or to other hoistable snippets.

But what if the snippet has `has_await` and references instance variables? The `can_hoist_snippet` function should return false because the instance variables have function depth > 0. So the snippet would be placed in `instance_level_snippets`.

OK, I think I need to look at the actual error more carefully. Let me search for "value" in the generated code where it might be undefined.

Actually, I just noticed something in the `create_derived` function:

```js
export function create_derived(state, expression, async = false) {
    const thunk = b.thunk(expression, async);
    
    if (async) {
        return b.call(b.await(b.call('$.save', b.call('$.async_derived', thunk))));
    } else {
        return b.call(state.analysis.runes ? '$.derived' : '$.derived_safe_equal', thunk);
    }
}
```

When `async` is true, it generates `(await $.save($.async_derived(thunk)))()`. This is used in `ConstTag`:

```js
let expression = create_derived(context.state, init, node.metadata.expression.has_await);
```

But wait, `create_derived` returns a `CallExpression` (the `()` call). This is assigned to a `const` variable. So the variable gets the value from the `save` function's returned function.

But what if `create_derived` is called with `async=true` but the surrounding function is NOT async? Then the `await` would be invalid JavaScript. Let me check if the SnippetBlock ensures the snippet function is async when the body has await.

In SnippetBlock:
```js
const has_await = node.body.metadata.has_await || false;
```

And then:
```js
let snippet = dev
    ? b.call('$.wrap_snippet', b.id(context.state.analysis.name), b.function(null, args, body, has_await))
    : b.arrow(args, body, has_await);
```

So the snippet function IS made async if `has_await` is true. Good.

But what if the snippet function is NOT made async but the body still contains an async-derived const? That would be a problem. But looking at the code, `has_await` is set from `node.body.metadata.has_await`, which should be true if the body contains an await expression.


This sets `has_await` on the current fragment. So if a snippet body contains an `await` expression, the snippet body's fragment should have `has_await = true`.

import 'svelte/internal/disclose-version';
import 'svelte/internal/flags/async';
import * as $ from 'svelte/internal/client';

export default function Test($$anchor) {
	const mySnippet = async ($$anchor) => {
		$.async_body(async () => {
			const value = (await $.save($.async_derived(() => promise)))();

			if ($.aborted()) return;

			$.next();

			var text = $.text();

			$.template_effect(() => $.set_text(text, $.get(value)));

		});
	};

	mySnippet($$anchor);

```js
export function async_derived(fn, location) {

    // ...
}
```

Let me look at the generated code more carefully:

```js
const mySnippet = async ($$anchor) => {
    $.async_body(async () => {
        const value = (await $.save($.async_derived(() => promise)))();
        if ($.aborted()) return;
        $.next();
        var text = $.text();

        $.append($$anchor, text);
    });

```

It is exported as `$.async_body`. But wait, `$.async_body` is used in the generated code. But `async_body` is an async function, and the generated code calls it without await. Let me check if that's the intended usage.

Looking at the Fragment visitor:
```js
if (has_await) {
    return b.block([b.stmt(b.call('$.async_body', b.arrow([], b.block(body), true)))]);
}
```

This generates:
```js
$.async_body(async () => {
    // body
});
```

This is a statement (not awaited). So `async_body` returns a Promise that's ignored. But `async_body` handles errors internally (via `try/catch` and `invoke_error_boundary`). So the Promise rejection should be handled.

But there's another issue: `async_body` calls `suspend()` and `unsuspend()`. These manage pending counts. If the Promise from `async_body` is not awaited, the code after it continues immediately, which might cause issues with the pending state.

But the specific error "value is not defined" doesn't seem to come from this.

Now let me think about what could cause "value is not defined".\n\nLooking at the generated code, I see `$.async_derived(() => promise)`. Here `() => promise` is a thunk that returns `promise`. When accessed directly, it returns the proxy object. `Promise.resolve(proxy)` would return a promise that resolves to the proxy (not the underlying Promise).\n\nWait, but `promise` was initialized with `$.proxy(Promise.resolve(\'hello\'))`. If the async handling of the argument somehow fails, `value` might not be defined when the snippet tries to use it.\n\nBut I already tested this case:\n```js\n$.async($$anchor, [() => promise], ($$anchor, $0) => {\n    mySnippet($$anchor, () => $.get($0));\n});\n```\n\nThis looks correct.\n\nOK, I\'m going to try a completely different approach. Fragment.js: replaced manual suspend/unsuspend with `$.async_body`\n3. transform-client.js: restructured how instance+template body is handled when has_await\n\nLet me check the Fragment.js change more carefully. Before:\n\n```js\nif (has_await) {\n    body.push(b.var(unsuspend, b.call(\'$.suspend\')));\n}\n// ... body ...\nif (has_await) {\n    return b.block([b.stmt(b.call(\'$.async_body\', b.arrow([], b.block(body), true)))]);\n} else {\n    return b.block(body);\n}\n```\n\nWait, I need to see the BEFORE version more carefully. The diff shows:\n\n```diff\n-\tif (has_await) {\n-\t\tbody.push(b.var(unsuspend, b.call(\'$.suspend\')));\n-\t}\n-\n\tbody.push(...state.consts);\n \n\tif (has_await) {\n\t\t// ...\n\t}\n \n-\tif (has_await) {\n-\t\tbody.push(b.stmt(b.call(unsuspend)));\n-\t}\n-\n-\treturn b.block(body);\n+\tif (has_await) {\n+\t\treturn b.block([b.stmt(b.call(\'$.async_body\', b.arrow([], b.block(body), true)))]);\n+\t} else {\n+\t\treturn b.block(body);\n+\t}\n```\n\nSo before, the body was wrapped in suspend/unsuspend manually:\n```js\nvar $$unsuspend = $.suspend();\n// ... body ...\n$$unsuspend();\n```\n\nAfter, it\'s wrapped in `$.async_body`:\n```js\n$.async_body(async () => {\n    // ... body ...\n});\n```\n\nThe key difference is that before, the suspend/unsuspend were synchronous, while now `async_body` is async. But wait, `async_body` is only used when `has_await` is true. If the body has await, the function needs to be async anyway.\n\nBut there\'s a subtle issue: `$.async_body` is an async function. It returns a Promise. But the generated code calls it without await:\n\n```js\n$.async_body(async () => { ... });\n```\n\nThis means the code after `$.async_body(...)` continues immediately, before the async body completes. The actual rendering happens later when the async body completes. But the caller (`mySnippet($$anchor)`) doesn\'t await `mySnippet`. So the rendering happens asynchronously.\n\nBut this is the same for the Fragment case:\n```js\n$.async_body(async () => {\n    // ... body ...\n});\n```\n\nWait, but in the component function:\n```js\nexport default function Test($$anchor) {\n    // ...\n    mySnippet($$anchor);\n}\n```\n\nThe component function calls `mySnippet`, which is async. But the component function doesn\'t await it. So the component function returns before the snippet\'s async body completes.\n\nBut is this a problem? The component function is called during mounting, which happens inside an effect. The effect might handle async work...\n\nActually, I think I finally found the issue! Let me look at the SnippetBlock more carefully. The SnippetBlock sets `has_await` on the snippet function:\n\n```js\nlet snippet = dev\n    ? b.call(\'$.wrap_snippet\', b.id(context.state.analysis.name), b.function(null, args, body, has_await))\n    : b.arrow(args, body, has_await);\n```\n\nSo the snippet function is async if `has_await` is true. But the snippet function body contains `$.async_body(...)`, which is another async operation. The snippet function is async, so you\'d expect it to `await` the `$.async_body` call. But it doesn\'t!\n\nLook at the generated code:\n```js\nconst mySnippet = async ($$anchor) => {\n    $.async_body(async () => {\n        // ...\n    });\n};\n```\n\nThe `$.async_body` is NOT awaited. So `mySnippet` returns immediately (resolved to undefined), and the actual body runs later. But the caller expects the snippet to have completed when it returns.\n\nBut actually, for the non-async snippet case, the snippet function is synchronous and completes immediately. For the async case, the snippet function is async but doesn\'t await its body. So the caller gets a Promise that resolves too early.\n\nHowever, the caller (`mySnippet($$anchor)`) doesn\'t await the result either. So both return immediately.\n\nThis might cause issues with the DOM not being updated in time, but I don\'t think it would cause "value is not defined".\n\nOK, I\'m going to try to look at this from yet another angle. Let me check if there\'s a regression specifically in how `SnippetBlock` interacts with the `Fragment` changes. Maybe the issue is that the SnippetBlock now wraps its body in `$.async_body` (via Fragment), but the SnippetBlock itself doesn\'t handle this correctly.\n\nWait, actually I just noticed something. Let me look at the SnippetBlock code again:\n\n```js\nconst block = /** @type {BlockStatement} */ (context.visit(node.body, child_state)).body;\nbody = b.block([\n    dev ? b.stmt(b.call(\'$.validate_snippet_args\', b.spread(b.id(\'arguments\')))) : b.empty,\n    ...declarations,\n    ...block\n]);\n```\n\nThe `context.visit(node.body, child_state)` visits the snippet body (a Fragment). The Fragment now returns `$.async_body(...)` if it has await. But the SnippetBlock wraps this in its own body. So the structure is:\n\n```\nasync ($$anchor) => {\n    // declarations\n    $.async_body(async () => {\n        // actual body content\n    });\n}\n```\n\nThe snippet function is async, but `$.async_body` is not awaited. So the snippet returns immediately.\n\nBut wait, before the Fragment changes (in 5.38.2), what would the output look like? The Fragment would have:\n\n```js\n// In Fragment output:\nvar $$unsuspend = $.suspend();\n// ... body content ...\n$$unsuspend();\n```\n\nAnd the SnippetBlock would wrap this in:\n```\nasync ($$anchor) => {\n    // declarations\n    var $$unsuspend = $.suspend();\n    // ... body content ...\n    $$unsuspend();\n}\n```\n\nThe snippet function is async, but the body content is synchronous (with suspend/unsuspend wrapped around it). The `await` expressions inside the body would be transformed to use `$.save()` and would be awaited. So the snippet function would properly wait for all awaits before returning.\n\nBut now, the body is wrapped in `$.async_body`, which is not awaited. So the snippet function doesn\'t wait for the body to complete.\n\nBut does this cause "value is not defined"? Let me think...\n\nIf the snippet function returns before the body completes, the caller might try to access something that hasn\'t been set up yet. But the caller (`mySnippet($$anchor)`) just calls the function and doesn\'t use its return value. The DOM manipulation happens inside the body.\n\nActually, maybe the issue is about variable scoping with hoisted snippets. Let me look at the hoisted case again:\n\n```js\nconst mySnippet = async ($$anchor) => {\n    $.async_body(async () => {\n        const value = (await $.save($.async_derived(load)))();\n        // ...\n    });\n};\n\nasync function load() {\n    return \'hello\';\n}\n\nexport default function Test($$anchor) {\n    mySnippet($$anchor);\n}\n```\n\nHere, `load` is at module level. `mySnippet` is at module level. `mySnippet` references `load` directly. This should work.\n\nBut what if `load` is an instance-level function and the snippet is hoisted? That can\'t happen because `can_hoist` would return false.\n\nOK, I think I\'ve been going around in circles. Let me look at the actual changes more carefully, specifically the `Memoizer.apply()` change:\n\nThe change was:\n```diff\n- return [...this.#async, ...this.#sync].map((memo, i) => {\n+ return [...this.#sync, ...this.#async].map((memo, i) => {\n```\n\nThis changes the order of ids. Before, async came first, then sync. After, sync comes first, then async.\n\nNow, in RenderTag:\n```js\nfor (let i = 0; i < call.arguments.length; i++) {\n    const arg = /** @type {Expression} */ (call.arguments[i]);\n    const metadata = node.metadata.arguments[i];\n    \n    let expression = build_expression(context, arg, metadata);\n    \n    if (metadata.has_await || metadata.has_call) {\n        expression = b.call(\'$.get\', memoizer.add(expression, metadata.has_await));\n    }\n    \n    args.push(b.thunk(expression));\n}\n\nmemoizer.apply();\n```\n\nHere, `memoizer.add` adds to `#sync` if `!has_await`, and to `#async` if `has_await`. OK so the generated code is consistent. Now let me think about what could cause "value is not defined".

Looking at the generated code, I see `$.async_derived(() => promise)`. Here `() => promise` is a thunk that returns `promise`. But `promise` is a `$state` variable initialized with `$.proxy(Promise.resolve('hello'))`. The `$.proxy` wraps the value in a proxy for reactivity. 

When `async_derived` calls this thunk, it gets `promise` which is the proxy. Then it awaits the result? No, `async_derived` expects the thunk to return a Promise:

```js
async_effect(() => {
    // ...
    try {
        var p = fn();
    } catch (error) {
        p = Promise.reject(error);
    }
    // ...
    var r = () => p;
    promise = prev?.then(r, r) ?? Promise.resolve(p);
    // ...
    promise.then(handler, (e) => handler(null, e || 'unknown'));
});
```

So `fn()` returns `promise` (the proxy). Then `Promise.resolve(p)` is called. If `p` is a Promise, `Promise.resolve(p)` returns that same promise. If `p` is not a Promise, it wraps it in a resolved promise.

But `promise` is a `$state` proxy, not an actual Promise. When accessed directly, it returns the proxy object. `Promise.resolve(proxy)` would return a promise that resolves to the proxy (not the underlying Promise).

Wait, but `promise` was initialized with `$.proxy(Promise.resolve('hello'))`. The `$.proxy` wraps the Promise. If you call `Promise.resolve($.proxy(somePromise))`, it returns the proxy, not the underlying Promise.

Actually, `Promise.resolve` checks if the argument is a thenable (has a `.then` method). The `$.proxy` wraps the original object, so it should still be thenable. So `Promise.resolve(proxy)` would return a promise that follows the proxy's `.then`, which should delegate to the original Promise's `.then`.

But wait, that's only if the proxy correctly proxies `.then`. Let me check if there's an issue with proxy access.

Actually, I think I'm overcomplicating this. Let me look at the actual error "value is not defined" from a different angle.

Let me look at what happens when `$.async_derived` throws an error because `active_effect` is null:

```js
export function async_derived(fn, location) {
    let parent = /** @type {Effect | null} */ (active_effect);
    if (parent === null) {
        e.async_derived_orphan();
    }
```

The error thrown is `async_derived_orphan`, not "value is not defined".

OK, let me look at the `save` function:

```js
export async function save(promise) {
    var restore = capture();
    var value = await promise;
    return () => {
        restore();
        return value;
    };
}
```

Here, `value` is a local variable inside `save`. If the promise rejects, `value` is never assigned, and the function throws. But the error would be the rejection error, not "value is not defined".

Wait, I just realized something. The `save` function has a local variable named `value`. But the generated code also uses `value` as a const name:

```js
const value = (await $.save($.async_derived(() => promise)))();
```

These are different scopes, so there's no conflict. The `value` inside `save` is not visible outside.

Hmm, let me look at the error from a completely different perspective. Maybe the issue is not about `{@const value = await promise}` but about something simpler like:

```svelte
{#snippet mySnippet(value)}
  {value}
{/snippet}

{@render mySnippet(await promise)}
```

Where `value` is a snippet parameter and `await promise` is an argument. If the async handling of the argument somehow fails, `value` might not be defined when the snippet tries to use it.

But I already tested this case:
```js

    mySnippet($$anchor, () => $.get($0));
});
```

This looks correct.

OK, I'm going to try a completely different approach. Let me look at what specific change between 5.38.2 and 5.38.3 could cause "value is not defined". The key changes are:

1. `Memoizer.apply()` order change: `#async, #sync` -> `#sync, #async`
2. Fragment.js: replaced manual suspend/unsuspend with `$.async_body`
3. transform-client.js: restructured how instance+template body is handled when has_await

Let me check the Fragment.js change more carefully. Before:

```js

    body.push(b.var(unsuspend, b.call('$.suspend')));
}
// ... body ...
if (has_await) {
    return b.block([b.stmt(b.call('$.async_body', b.arrow([], b.block(body), true)))]);

    return b.block(body);
}
```

Wait, I need to see the BEFORE version more carefully. The diff shows:

```diff
-	if (has_await) {
-		body.push(b.var(unsuspend, b.call('$.suspend')));
-	}
-
	body.push(...state.consts);

		// ...
	}
 
-	if (has_await) {
-		body.push(b.stmt(b.call(unsuspend)));
-	}

+	if (has_await) {
+		return b.block([b.stmt(b.call('$.async_body', b.arrow([], b.block(body), true)))]);
+	} else {
+		return b.block(body);
+	}
```

So before, the body was wrapped in suspend/unsuspend manually:
```js

// ... body ...
$$unsuspend();
```

After, it's wrapped in `$.async_body`:
```js
$.async_body(async () => {
    // ... body ...
});
```

The key difference is that before, the suspend/unsuspend were synchronous, while now `async_body` is async. But wait, `async_body` is only used when `has_await` is true. If the body has await, the function needs to be async anyway.

But there's a subtle issue: `$.async_body` is an async function. It returns a Promise. But the generated code calls it without await:

```js
$.async_body(async () => { ... });
```

This means the code after `$.async_body(...)` continues immediately, before the async body completes. In the Fragment case, this might be fine because the fragment is the last thing in the block. But in the SnippetBlock case:

```js

    $.async_body(async () => {
        // ... body ...
    });
};
```

`mySnippet` calls `$.async_body` and then returns (resolves to undefined). The actual rendering happens later when the async body completes. But the caller (`mySnippet($$anchor)`) doesn't await `mySnippet`. So the rendering happens asynchronously.

But this is the same for the Fragment case:
```js
$.async_body(async () => {
    // ... body ...
});
```

Wait, but in the component function:
```js
export default function Test($$anchor) {
    // ...
    mySnippet($$anchor);
}
```

The component function calls `mySnippet`, which is async. But the component function doesn't await it. So the component function returns before the snippet's async body completes.

But is this a problem? The component function is called during mounting, which happens inside an effect. The effect might handle async work...

Actually, I think I finally found the issue! Let me look at the SnippetBlock more carefully. The SnippetBlock sets `has_await` on the snippet function:

```js
let snippet = dev
    ? b.call('$.wrap_snippet', b.id(context.state.analysis.name), b.function(null, args, body, has_await))
    : b.arrow(args, body, has_await);
```

So the snippet function is async if `has_await` is true. But the snippet function body contains `$.async_body(...)`, which is another async operation. The snippet function is async, so you'd expect it to `await` the `$.async_body` call. But it doesn't!

Look at the generated code:
```js
const mySnippet = async ($$anchor) => {
    $.async_body(async () => {
        // ...
    });
};
```

The `$.async_body` is NOT awaited. So `mySnippet` returns immediately (resolved to undefined), and the actual body runs later. But the caller expects the snippet to have completed when it returns.

But actually, for the non-async snippet case, the snippet function is synchronous and completes immediately. For the async case, the snippet function is async but doesn't await its body. So the caller gets a Promise that resolves too early.

However, the caller (`mySnippet($$anchor)`) doesn't await the result either. So both return immediately.

This might cause issues with the DOM not being updated in time, but I don't think it would cause "value is not defined".

OK, I'm going to try to look at this from yet another angle. Let me check if there's a regression specifically in how `SnippetBlock` interacts with the `Fragment` changes. Maybe the issue is that the SnippetBlock now wraps its body in `$.async_body` (via Fragment), but the SnippetBlock itself doesn't handle this correctly.

Wait, actually I just noticed something. Let me look at the SnippetBlock code again:

```js

body = b.block([
    dev ? b.stmt(b.call('$.validate_snippet_args', b.spread(b.id('arguments')))) : b.empty,
    ...declarations,
    ...block

```

The `context.visit(node.body, child_state)` visits the snippet body (a Fragment). The Fragment now returns `$.async_body(...)` if it has await. But the SnippetBlock wraps this in its own body. So the structure is:

```

    // declarations
    $.async_body(async () => {
        // actual body content
    });

```

The snippet function is async, but `$.async_body` is not awaited. So the snippet returns immediately.

But wait, before the Fragment changes (in 5.38.2), what would the output look like? The Fragment would have:


And the SnippetBlock would wrap this in:
```
async ($$anchor) => {
    // declarations

    $$unsuspend();

```

The snippet function is async, but the body content is synchronous (with suspend/unsuspend wrapped around it). The `await` expressions inside the body would be transformed to use `$.save()` and would be awaited. So the snippet function would properly wait for all awaits before returning.

But now, the body is wrapped in `$.async_body`, which is not awaited. So the snippet function doesn't wait for the body to complete.

But does this cause "value is not defined"? Let me think...

If the snippet function returns before the body completes, the caller might try to access something that hasn't been set up yet. But the caller (`mySnippet($$anchor)`) just calls the function and doesn't use its return value. The DOM manipulation happens inside the body.

Actually, maybe the issue is about variable scoping with hoisted snippets. Let me look at the hoisted case again:

Here, `load` is at module level. `mySnippet` is at module level. `mySnippet` references `load` directly. This should work.

OK, I think I've been going around in circles. Let me look at the actual changes more carefully, specifically the `Memoizer.apply()` change:

The change was:
```diff
- return [...this.#async, ...this.#sync].map((memo, i) => {
+ return [...this.#sync, ...this.#async].map((memo, i) => {
```

This changes the order of ids. Before, async came first, then sync. After, sync comes first, then async.

Now, in RenderTag:
```js
for (let i = 0; i < call.arguments.length; i++) {
    const arg = /** @type {Expression} */ (call.arguments[i]);
    const metadata = node.metadata.arguments[i];
    
    let expression = build_expression(context, arg, metadata);
    
    if (metadata.has_await || metadata.has_call) {
        expression = b.call('$.get', memoizer.add(expression, metadata.has_await));
    }
    
    args.push(b.thunk(expression));
}

memoizer.apply();
```

Here, `memoizer.add` adds to `#sync` if `!has_await`, and to `#async` if `has_await`. The `apply()` then assigns ids based on the order.

But then:
```js
const statements = memoizer.deriveds(context.state.analysis.runes);
```

`deriveds` returns declarations for `#sync` items. And:
```js
const async_values = memoizer.async_values();
```

`async_values` returns thunks for `#async` items.

Then:
```js
if (async_values) {
    context.state.init.push(
        b.stmt(
            b.call(
                '$.async',
                context.state.node,
                memoizer.async_values(),
                b.arrow([context.state.node, ...memoizer.async_ids()], b.block(statements))
            )
        )
    );
}
```

The `$.async` callback receives `async_ids` as parameters. The `statements` contain the `deriveds` (sync) declarations.

Now, the `args` array contains thunks like `() => $.get($0)` where `$0` is the id assigned by `apply()`. If the order of ids changes, the mapping between args and the actual values could be wrong.

Wait, but the `args` are built BEFORE `apply()` is called. The `memoizer.add` returns an id with placeholder name `#`. Later, `apply()` sets the real names. So the `args` reference the same id objects.

Let me trace through an example with 2 arguments: first is sync, second is async.

Before the fix:
- Iteration 1: `has_await=false`, added to `#sync` -> id object with name `#` (let's call it `id_sync`)
- Iteration 2: `has_await=true`, added to `#async` -> id object with name `#` (let's call it `id_async`)
- `args = [() => $.get(id_sync), () => $.get(id_async)]`
- `apply()`: `[...#async, ...#sync]` = `[id_async, id_sync]` -> names: `id_async` -> `$0`, `id_sync` -> `$1`
- `deriveds()`: returns `[let $1 = $.derived(...)]` (for id_sync)
- `async_ids()`: returns `[id_async]` (which is `$0`)
- `$.async(node, [async_thunk], ($anchor, $0) => { let $1 = ...; ... })`
- But `args` references `id_sync` (`$1`) and `id_async` (`$0`)
- The snippet call: `mySnippet($anchor, () => $.get($1), () => $.get($0))`

Wait, that's wrong! The `args` were built in the order of the arguments: first sync, then async. So `args[0]` is `() => $.get($1)` (sync), `args[1]` is `() => $.get($0)` (async).

But the `$.async` callback has parameters `($anchor, $0)` where `$0` is the async id. The sync id `$1` is declared as a variable.

So the snippet is called with:
- First argument: `() => $.get($1)` - reads the sync derived
- Second argument: `() => $.get($0)` - reads the async value

This looks correct! The ids match up.

Now after the fix:
- `apply()`: `[...#sync, ...#async]` = `[id_sync, id_async]` -> names: `id_sync` -> `$0`, `id_async` -> `$1`
- `deriveds()`: returns `[let $0 = $.derived(...)]` (for id_sync)
- `async_ids()`: returns `[id_async]` (which is `$1`)
- `$.async(node, [async_thunk], ($anchor, $1) => { let $0 = ...; ... })`
- `args`: `[() => $.get($0), () => $.get($1)]`

So the snippet is called with:
- First argument: `() => $.get($0)` - reads the sync derived
- Second argument: `() => $.get($1)` - reads the async value

This also looks correct!

So the Memoizer order change shouldn't cause "value is not defined".

Let me check if there's an issue with the `build_render_statement` function that also uses `Memoizer`:

```js
export function build_render_statement(state) {
    const { memoizer } = state;
    const ids = memoizer.apply();
    return b.stmt(
        b.call(
            '$.template_effect',
            b.arrow(
                ids,
                state.update.length === 1 && state.update[0].type === 'ExpressionStatement'
                    ? state.update[0].expression
                    : b.block(state.update)
            ),
            memoizer.sync_values(),
            memoizer.async_values()
        )
    );
}
```

Here, `ids` is the result of `apply()`, which is `[...#sync, ...#async]` after the fix. The arrow function takes `ids` as parameters. And `sync_values` and `async_values` are passed to `template_effect`.

In `template_effect`:
```js
export function template_effect(fn, sync = [], async = []) {
    flatten(sync, async, (values) => {
        // values = [...sync.map(d), ...result]
        fn(...values);
    });
}
```

So `values` is `[...sync_deriveds, ...async_resolved_values]`. And `fn` is called with these values spread.

The arrow function parameters are `ids` which is `[...sync_ids, ...async_ids]`. So the first sync id receives the first sync value, etc.

This should work correctly after the fix (sync first, then async).

But before the fix, `apply()` returned `[...#async, ...#sync]`, so `ids` was `[...async_ids, ...sync_ids]`. And `flatten` passes `[...sync_values, ...async_values]`. So the first async id would receive the first sync value! This was the bug that the fix addressed.

But this bug would cause values to be swapped, not "value is not defined".

2026-05-30 08:37:33,976 - INFO - swea-agent-sveltejs__svelte-16666 - 💭 THOUGHT
I see there are branches with various fixes. Let me look at the PR number in the PR description. The PR title mentions a bug. Let me check if there's a specific commit that fixes this issue.

Actually, I notice that the HEAD is at `svelte@5.38.3`. The PR says this was working in `5.38.2` and broken in `5.38.3`. So the bug was introduced in one of the commits between these two versions.

Let me look at the `a60995abe` commit more carefully - it's the "fix: allow async `{@const}` in more places" commit. This change modified how `ConstTag` works inside snippets for async mode. But it also changed how `Fragment` works (using `async_body`).

Wait, actually the Fragment.js change was in `1dcced59e` (fix: properly catch top level await errors). Let me check if that change could cause the issue.

Looking at the Fragment.js change again:

Before:
```js
if (has_await) {
    body.push(b.var(unsuspend, b.call('$.suspend')));
}
body.push(...state.consts);
// ...
if (has_await) {
    body.push(b.stmt(b.call(unsuspend)));
}
return b.block(body);
```

After:
```js
body.push(...state.consts);
// ...
if (has_await) {
    return b.block([b.stmt(b.call('$.async_body', b.arrow([], b.block(body), true)))]);
} else {
    return b.block(body);
}
```

The key difference: before, the `state.consts` were inside the suspend/unsuspend block. After, they're still inside the async_body block (since the whole body is wrapped). But the `state.consts` are the `{@const}` declarations. These are now placed inside the async arrow function.

But wait, there's another important difference: before, the `body` was just statements that were executed synchronously (with suspend/unsuspend around them). The `await` expressions inside would be transformed to use `$.save()`, which would pause execution. But the function containing this body was async (because `has_await` was true at the SnippetBlock level).

Now, the body is wrapped in `$.async_body`, which is an async function. But the SnippetBlock still makes the snippet function async. So the snippet function is async, and inside it, `$.async_body` is called (without await).

I think the issue might be that `$.async_body` is not awaited inside the snippet function. This means the snippet function returns immediately, and any code that depends on the snippet's content being rendered might fail.

But more importantly, I just noticed something: the `declarations` in SnippetBlock are placed BEFORE the `block` (which comes from Fragment). So the structure is:

```js
async ($$anchor) => {
    // declarations (for destructured params)
    $.async_body(async () => {
        // consts and init and update from Fragment
    });
}
```

But the `declarations` are inside the async snippet function, outside the `async_body` wrapper. If the `declarations` contain any reference to variables that are only available inside the `async_body` callback, that would be fine because they're in the same closure scope.

But what if the `declarations` themselves need to be async? They don't, because they're only for destructured snippet parameters.

OK, I'm going to look at this from a completely different angle. Let me search for where the error "value is not defined" might originate from in the Svelte runtime:

```js
    var value = await promise;
```

```js

    var previous_reaction = active_reaction;

        set_active_reaction(previous_reaction);
```
